critic_raw:
This CNN vs. traditional image processing diabetic retinopathy detection experiment has four methodological issues.

1. Normalization statistics computed on full dataset before splitting is data leakage. 'All images are resized to 512×512 pixels and normalized using per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the full dataset statistics prior to splitting.' With 50,000 images, this means validation and test image statistics inform the normalization applied during training. The design makes no argument that training-set-only statistics would be unstable with 30,000 training images. Evidence: refit normalization on training images only; verify test sensitivity does not change materially. If it does, the normalization leakage had a substantive effect.

2. Sensitivity at a fixed specificity threshold set on the validation set may not be achievable on the test set without threshold adjustment. The design states 'A threshold is chosen on the validation set to achieve this specificity target; the same threshold is applied to the test set without modification.' If the CNN's score distribution on the test set differs from the validation set (which is expected due to distributional variation across clinics and time), the threshold calibrated for ≥0.95 specificity on validation may produce lower specificity on the test set. Reporting sensitivity 'at ≥0.95 specificity' while using a fixed threshold is misleading — the specificity constraint may not be met on the test set. Evidence: report actual test-set specificity achieved at the validation-derived threshold; if test specificity is below 0.95, report that the primary metric target was not met.

3. The classical feature extraction baseline relies on automated algorithms (Hough circle transform, morphological closing, medial axis skeleton) that have known failure modes on low-quality retinal images. The design includes quality screening and excludes non-diagnostic images, but the specific failure modes of these classical algorithms — e.g., poor optic disc detection in images with heavy drusen or neovascularization, microaneurysm undersegmentation in low-contrast images — are exactly the cases where DR severity is clinically significant. If the baseline's classical algorithms fail on images where DR is present and hard to detect, the baseline's recall on positive cases is artificially depressed, making the CNN comparison easier than it would be with a more robust classical method. Evidence: report classical feature extraction algorithm success rate (fraction of images for which each feature was successfully computed) stratified by disease severity; if extraction failure rates are higher for severe DR cases, the baseline is handicapped by algorithm failures, not by inherent feature limitations.

4. The temporal split strategy is mixed with clinic-site stratification in a way that creates an uncontrolled confound. 'Within each stratum [of clinic site and prevalence], images are sorted chronologically and split as follows: Training (60%, months 0-14), Validation (20%, months 15-18), Test (20%, months 19-24).' If different clinics joined the study at different times, 'month 0' represents different calendar dates for different clinics. A temporal split where the training-validation-test boundary is relative to each clinic's start date, rather than absolute calendar dates, means images from different clinics at the same calendar date can appear in different splits. This confounds any temporal analysis — 'earlier' and 'later' are clinic-relative, not calendar-time-relative. Evidence: verify whether temporal boundaries are defined in absolute calendar months or relative to each clinic's enrollment start; if relative, reconcile this with the claim that temporal ordering prevents future information leakage.
all_issues_raised: Per-channel normalization statistics computed on full 50,000-image dataset before splitting — data leakage into training normalization
Threshold calibrated on validation set for ≥0.95 specificity may not achieve ≥0.95 specificity on test set — primary metric target potentially unmet
Classical feature extraction algorithms have known failure modes on severe DR cases — baseline may be handicapped by extraction failures on the hardest positive cases
Temporal split boundaries may be clinic-relative rather than calendar-absolute — temporal ordering claim may be confounded by staggered clinic enrollment
Normalization computed on full dataset before splitting leaks test set distributional characteristics into training
CNN receives full raw image with ImageNet pretraining while baseline receives five hand-crafted features; comparison tests representation richness, not architecture
Sensitivity threshold selected at >=0.95 specificity on validation may not achieve that specificity on test set, making reported sensitivity non-comparable
Temporal split within sparse strata may produce validation and test sets with too few images for meaningful evaluation
Classical feature extraction algorithms are not validated for accuracy on this specific dataset; features may be noise for certain image types
CNN normalization statistics computed on full dataset before splitting constitutes preprocessing leakage, violating the temporal split rationale
Threshold transfer from validation-trained model to combined-trained model may not maintain the ≥0.95 specificity constraint on the test set
No augmentation for CNN fine-tuning risks inadequate domain adaptation from ImageNet to fundus photographs; failure may be misattributed to architectural inadequacy
Classical feature extraction algorithm parameters (thresholds, kernel sizes) not specified as tuned or fixed; uncontrolled calibration may handicap baseline
